Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: 
Living in Christ’s Victory Today and Forever 
 VOLUME 3 DR. DANIEL FOLARIN FOREWORD BY REV. MAXWELL OGAGA
1

SOTERIA & VICTORIOUS ESCHATOLOGY: LIVING IN CHRIST’S VICTORY TODAY AND FOREVER
 
 VOLUME 3

ACKNOWLEDGEMENT 
This work is born out of deep gratitude for the faithful labors of those who 
have
 
gone
 
before,
 
men
 
and
 
women
 
of
 
whom
 
the
 
world
 
was
 
not
 
worthy.
 
I
 
give
 
thanks
 
to
 
God
 
for
 
the
 
saints;
 
pastors,
 
teachers,
 
mentors,
 
and
 
friends,
 
who
 
have
 
devoted
 
themselves
 
to
 
the
 
sacred
 
task
 
of
 
teaching
 
the
 
Word
 
of
 
Truth
 
with
 
clarity,
 
courage,
 
and
 
compassion.
 
Their
 
unwavering
 
commitment
 
to
 
the
 
Gospel
 
has
 
not
 
only
 
shaped
 
my
 
understanding
 
but
 
has
 
kindled
 
in
 
me
 
a
 
fire
 
to
 
carry
 
this
 
message
 
further.
 
I also stand on the shoulders of the fathers of the faith; apostolic voices, 
early
 
church
 
fathers,
 
Reformers,
 
Puritans,
 
and
 
modern
 
theologians
 
who,
 
though
 
separated
 
by
 
time
 
and
 
language,
 
have
 
proclaimed
 
with
 
one
 
voice
 
the
 
supremacy
 
of
 
Christ,
 
the
 
victory
 
of
 
His
 
cross,
 
and
 
the
 
hope
 
of
 
His
 
return.
 
Their
 
writings
 
and
 
sacrifices
 
have
 
laid
 
the
 
theological
 
and
 
spiritual
 
foundation
 
upon
 
which
 
this
 
work
 
seeks
 
humbly
 
to
 
build.
 
To the unnamed saints who contended for this truth in prayer, in preaching, 
in
 
exile,
 
and
 
in
 
martyrdom,
 
your
 
labor
 
is
 
not
 
in
 
vain.
 
This
 
book
 
is,
 
in
 
part,
 
your
 
legacy.
 
May the truths proclaimed in these pages advance the Gospel of the 
Kingdom
 
and
 
prepare
 
the
 
Church
 
for
 
her
 
full
 
inheritance
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Soli Deo Gloria. 
 
3

DEDICATION 
 
To every heart that longs for His appearing, 
And
 
humbly
 
bends
 
to
 
receive
 
the
 
Word,
 
May
 
you,
 
with
 
grace,
 
unlearn
 
and
 
relearn,
 
In
 
the
 
journey
 
of
 
faith,
 
where
 
wisdom
 
is
 
stirred.
 
May
 
His
 
great
 
salvation
 
unfold
 
in
 
your
 
soul,
 
A
 
timeless
 
story,
 
forever
 
whole
 
 
 
 
 
4

FOREWARD 
The book Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: Living in Christ’s Victory 
Today
 
and
 
Forever
 
presents
 
a
 
fresh
 
scriptural
 
perspective
 
to
 
the
 
subject
 
of
 
the
 
all-encompassing
 
nature
 
of
 
salvation.
 
Dr. Daniel, like a wise master builder (1 Corinthians 3:10), has skillfully 
explored
 
the
 
themes
 
on
 
salvation
 
with
 
a
 
present
 
truth
 
mindset
 
of
 
a
 
victorious
 
eschatological
 
perspective.
 
I
 
agree
 
with
 
Dr.
 
Folarin:
 
“
If
 
God
 
has
 
not
 
given
 
us
 
the
 
spirit
 
of
 
fear
”
 
(2
 
Timothy
 
1),
 
then
 
we
 
cannot
 
have
 
a
 
fear-based
 
eschatology.
 
Our
 
view
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
work
 
must
 
be
 
laced
 
in
 
hope
 
and
 
victory.
 
The work may rattle religious minds, but that’s exactly what Jesus came to 
do.
 
The
 
religious
 
order
 
of
 
His
 
day
 
could
 
not
 
comprehend
 
what
 
Jesus
 
was
 
teaching,
 
not
 
because
 
it
 
was
 
not
 
the
 
truth,
 
but
 
because
 
they
 
were
 
fixated
 
on
 
their
 
interpretation
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Scriptures
 
and
 
were
 
not
 
willing
 
to
 
see
 
what
 
Jesus
 
was
 
saying.
 
Yet
 
these
 
scriptures
 
testify
 
of
 
Him
 
(John
 
5:39).
 
What
 
this
 
book
 
offers
 
is
 
pointing
 
us
 
back
 
to
 
scriptures.
 
 
Christ's work on the cross, the holistic and dynamic effect of salvation was 
thoroughly
 
explained
 
in
 
this
 
book.
 
Expounding
 
on
 
Paul’s
 
theology
 
of
 
salvation,
 
this
 
work
 
elucidates
 
the
 
correlation
 
between
 
Christ
 
and
 
the
 
cosmos
 
bringing
 
a
 
clear
 
perspective
 
to
 
Colossians
 
1:20
 
&
 
Romans
 
8:19-20.
 
Furthermore, key concepts that would frame our worldview are adequately 
explained
 
in
 
this
 
volume:
 
Matthew
 
24,
 
the
 
role
 
of
 
Israel,
 
“shortly
 
and
 
at
 
hand”,
 
the
 
“New
 
Heaven
 
and
 
the
 
New
 
Earth.”
 
The
 
inability
 
to
 
understand
 
these
 
terms
 
in
 
the
 
light
 
of
 
the
 
finished
 
work
 
of
 
Christ
 
and
 
their
 
biblical
 
context
 
has
 
hindered
 
us
 
from
 
walking
 
in
 
the
 
triumphant
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
of
 
God.
 
In addition, the practical application section of the book makes it one of the 
finest
 
I
 
have
 
read
 
on
 
these
 
subjects,
 
with
 
particular
 
attention
 
paid
 
to
 
how
 
5

the truths can be applied in our present-day reality today for the man in 
Christ.
 
This book ends with a greater perspective on the Great Commission and 
the
 
mission
 
of
 
advancing
 
the
 
kingdom
 
of
 
God,
 
which
 
encompasses
 
all
 
domains
 
of
 
influence.
 
The
 
salt
 
of
 
the
 
earth
 
and
 
the
 
light
 
of
 
the
 
world
 
takes
 
on
 
a
 
greater
 
meaning
 
and
 
leads
 
us
 
back
 
to
 
God’s
 
original
 
intent
 
in
 
Genesis
 
1:26
 
for
 
man.
 
The work is theologically enriching and practical enough for application. 
Whether
 
you
 
are
 
a
 
student
 
of
 
theology
 
seeking
 
to
 
go
 
deeper
 
in
 
your
 
search
 
of
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
God’s
 
word,
 
or
 
you
 
are
 
a
 
new
 
believer
 
finding
 
your
 
way
 
around
 
the
 
truths
 
of
 
salvation,
 
the
 
finished
 
work
 
of
 
Christ,
 
and
 
eschatology,
 
this
 
book
 
would
 
be
 
a
 
handy
 
companion.
 
 
Maxwell E. Ogaga 
Author, Pastor & Bible Teacher Pastoral Overseer, God’s Kingdom Advancement Network of Churches, 
Nigeria
 President, WordConnect International & Dear Timothy Ministry Leaders 
Academy
 
 
6

PREFACE 
Two years ago, in 2023, I began a journey of teaching about Victorious 
Eschatology.
 
What
 
started
 
as
 
a
 
deep
 
dive
 
into
 
the
 
hope-filled,
 
victorious
 
view
 
of
 
the
 
Kingdom
 
of
 
God
 
soon
 
turned
 
into
 
a
 
significant
 
turning
 
point
 
in
 
my
 
ministry.
 
The
 
series
 
was
 
long,
 
thorough,
 
and,
 
I
 
believe,
 
enlightening
 
for
 
many.
 
It
 
shed
 
light
 
on
 
perspectives
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
plan
 
that
 
many
 
had
 
not
 
previously
 
considered,
 
offering
 
fresh
 
insights
 
into
 
the
 
way
 
we
 
should
 
understand
 
and
 
engage
 
with
 
the
 
Scriptures,
 
especially
 
in
 
relation
 
to
 
the
 
eschatological
 
promises
 
of
 
God.
 
However, the journey was not without its challenges. While many in the 
congregation
 
embraced
 
the
 
teachings
 
with
 
an
 
open
 
heart,
 
seeking
 
to
 
understand
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
God’s
 
victory
 
over
 
all
 
things,
 
others
 
found
 
these
 
new
 
insights
 
to
 
be
 
offensive.
 
The
 
change
 
in
 
perspective
 
was
 
unsettling
 
for
 
some,
 
particularly
 
those
 
who
 
had
 
adhered
 
to
 
more
 
traditional
 
interpretations
 
of
 
prophecy.
 
In
 
fact,
 
this
 
teaching
 
led
 
to
 
a
 
difficult
 
season
 
in
 
the
 
church,
 
as
 
some
 
members,
 
shaken
 
by
 
the
 
new
 
ideas,
 
chose
 
to
 
leave.
 
It
 
was
 
a
 
hard
 
season,
 
one
 
that
 
tested
 
my
 
resolve
 
and
 
stretched
 
me
 
in
 
ways
 
I
 
had
 
not
 
anticipated.
 
The
 
emotional
 
toll
 
of
 
losing
 
members
 
who
 
had
 
been
 
deeply
 
involved
 
in
 
the
 
ministry
 
was
 
felt
 
deeply,
 
and
 
I
 
found
 
myself
 
questioning
 
not
 
just
 
the
 
direction
 
of
 
the
 
church
 
but
 
my
 
own
 
calling
 
as
 
a
 
pastor.
 
Yet, in the midst of this turmoil, I made a firm resolution: I would not 
retreat
 
from
 
the
 
truth
 
that
 
God
 
had
 
revealed.
 
In
 
fact,
 
I
 
became
 
more
 
committed
 
than
 
ever
 
to
 
amplifying
 
the
 
message
 
of
 
Victorious
 
Eschatology,
 
because
 
I
 
believe
 
this
 
truth
 
is
 
essential
 
for
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ
 
today.
 
The
 
dispensationalist
 
view,
 
which
 
has
 
dominated
 
much
 
of
 
the
 
church
 
world
 
in
 
recent
 
times,
 
has,
 
in
 
my
 
view,
 
caused
 
significant
 
damage,
 
fostering
 
fear,
 
division,
 
and
 
an
 
unbiblical
 
sense
 
of
 
helplessness
 
among
 
believers.
 
It
 
presents
 
a
 
view
 
of
 
the
 
end
 
times
 
that
 
undermines
 
the
 
victorious
 
reality
 
that
 
Scripture
 
points
 
us
 
to.
 
7

What I’ve come to realize is that Victorious Eschatology, the belief that 
Christ
 
has
 
already
 
secured
 
victory
 
over
 
sin,
 
death,
 
and
 
the
 
powers
 
of
 
evil,
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
new
 
or
 
novel
 
idea.
 
In
 
fact,
 
it
 
is
 
deeply
 
rooted
 
in
 
church
 
history.
 
The
 
early
 
church
 
fathers
 
upheld
 
this
 
view,
 
and
 
even
 
great
 
preachers
 
like
 
Charles
 
Spurgeon
 
maintained
 
a
 
sense
 
of
 
victory
 
in
 
their
 
eschatological
 
outlook.
 
This
 
message
 
of
 
the
 
already-but-not-yet
 
reign
 
of
 
Christ,
 
where
 
His
 
victory
 
is
 
both
 
certain
 
and
 
present,
 
is
 
not
 
foreign
 
to
 
our
 
Christian
 
heritage.
 
In writing this book, I am offering my contribution to the body of Christ, 
adding
 
my
 
voice
 
to
 
a
 
growing
 
chorus
 
of
 
those
 
who
 
are
 
calling
 
the
 
church
 
back
 
to
 
a
 
biblical
 
and
 
victorious
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
Kingdom.
 
My
 
heart
 
is
 
to
 
see
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ
 
living
 
in
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
what
 
has
 
already
 
been
 
accomplished
 
through
 
the
 
finished
 
work
 
of
 
Christ,
 
not
 
waiting
 
in
 
fear
 
and
 
defeat,
 
but
 
standing
 
in
 
boldness,
 
reigning
 
with
 
Christ
 
now,
 
and
 
walking
 
in
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
salvation.
 
While this journey has been really personal for me, I trust that it is also part 
of
 
a
 
larger
 
movement
 
within
 
the
 
Church,
 
one
 
that
 
will
 
see
 
believers
 
stepping
 
into
 
their
 
God-given
 
authority,
 
embracing
 
the
 
hope
 
of
 
the
 
Gospel,
 
and
 
proclaiming
 
the
 
victorious
 
reign
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
in
 
the
 
world
 
today.
 
This
 
book
 
is
 
my
 
humble
 
offering,
 
a
 
voice
 
among
 
many
 
who
 
are
 
committed
 
to
 
restoring
 
the
 
victorious
 
vision
 
of
 
God’s
 
Kingdom.
 
I invite you to read this with an open heart and a willingness to explore the 
Scriptures
 
in
 
a
 
fresh
 
light.
 
May
 
this
 
message
 
challenge
 
you,
 
encourage
 
you,
 
and
 
ultimately
 
transform
 
the
 
way
 
you
 
view
 
not
 
only
 
the
 
future
 
but
 
also
 
the
 
present
 
reign
 
of
 
Christ
 
in
 
your
 
life.
 
Amen!
 
 
Grace and Peace! 
Dr. Daniel Folarin 
8

INTRODUCTION 
Welcome to Volume 3 of the Soteria series. If you’ve been with me 
through
 
the
 
first
 
two
 
volumes,
 
Soteria:
 
The
 
Unbreakable
 
Gift
 
of
 
Sonship
 
and
 
Redemption
 
and
 
Soteria
 
and
 
Rest:
 
The
 
Story
 
of
 
Redemption
 
from
 
Eden
 
to
 
Eternity
,
 
you’ve
 
already
 
begun
 
to
 
explore
 
the
 
profound
 
depth
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
and
 
the
 
salvation
 
He
 
offers.
 
Now,
 
in
 
this
 
third
 
volume,
 
we’re
 
stepping
 
into
 
a
 
new
 
and
 
exciting
 
chapter:
 
Victorious
 
Eschatology
.
 
It’s
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
theological
 
exploration,
 
but
 
an
 
invitation
 
to
 
live
 
in
 
the
 
victory
 
that
 
Christ
 
has
 
already
 
secured
 
for
 
us.
 
I want to be transparent: over the years, I’ve come to realize that prophecy, 
especially
 
when
 
discussed
 
in
 
church,
 
can
 
often
 
feel
 
distant
 
or
 
mysterious.
 
But
 
it
 
doesn’t
 
have
 
to
 
be
 
that
 
way.
 
In
 
the
 
biblical
 
sense,
 
prophecy
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
about
 
predicting
 
the
 
future;
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
divine
 
unveiling
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan,
 
ultimately
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
In
 
this
 
book,
 
I
 
invite
 
you
 
to
 
draw
 
near
 
to
 
that
 
unfolding
 
plan
 
and
 
to
 
behold
 
Jesus,
 
the
 
Savior
 
who
 
reigns
 
in
 
glory.
 
This
 
is
 
a
 
call
 
to
 
walk
 
boldly
 
in
 
the
 
transformative
 
power
 
of
 
Soteria
,
 
the
 
great
 
salvation
 
of
 
God.
 
This isn’t just about theology; it’s a personal invitation. As believers, we 
are
 
at
 
the
 
crossroads
 
of
 
promises
 
once
 
foretold
 
and
 
their
 
ongoing
 
fulfillment
 
in
 
Christ.
 
What
 
was
 
once
 
promised
 
in
 
the
 
scriptures
 
has
 
already
 
been
 
accomplished
 
through
 
His
 
finished
 
work.
 
What
 
we
 
now
 
live
 
in
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
future
 
hope,
 
but
 
a
 
present
 
reality
 
that
 
we
 
get
 
to
 
experience
 
today.
 
In these pages, I will walk with you through the beauty of Victorious 
Eschatology
,
 
God’s
 
plan
 
for
 
the
 
fulfillment
 
of
 
salvation
 
that
 
transcends
 
time,
 
sin,
 
and
 
even
 
death.
 
Together,
 
we’ll
 
explore
 
how
 
this
 
victory
 
isn’t
 
something
 
to
 
be
 
merely
 
observed
 
in
 
the
 
future,
 
but
 
something
 
we
 
can
 
actively
 
live
 
in
 
today.
 
9

The call is clear: we are meant to reign with Christ, not only in the future, 
but
 
now.
 
And
 
as
 
we
 
do,
 
we
 
find
 
that
 
His
 
victory
 
over
 
sin,
 
death,
 
and
 
time
 
is
 
meant
 
to
 
empower
 
us
 
to
 
live
 
as
 
more
 
than
 
conquerors.
 
As you read, I pray that you will discover not just the theological richness 
of
 
salvation,
 
but
 
also
 
a
 
roadmap
 
for
 
how
 
to
 
live
 
it
 
out.
 
This
 
book
 
is
 
for
 
anyone
 
who
 
is
 
ready
 
to
 
challenge
 
old
 
ways
 
of
 
thinking,
 
to
 
embrace
 
a
 
deeper
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purposes,
 
and
 
to
 
allow
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word
 
to
 
shape
 
their
 
lives.
 
For those who long to know Jesus more intimately and to live empowered 
by
 
the
 
work
 
He
 
has
 
already
 
completed,
 
I
 
offer
 
you
 
this
 
invitation:
 
let’s
 
reign
 
with
 
boldness,
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
salvation,
 
and
 
hold
 
firmly
 
to
 
the
 
hope
 
that
 
will
 
never
 
disappoint.
 
 
 
10

CONTENT PART ONE: The Eternal Blueprint of Salvation Chapter 1: Chosen Before Time Began 15 ● God’s eternal purpose in Christ (Eph. 1:4–5) ● Salvation not as reaction, but as preordained (2 Tim. 1:9) ● Christ crucified from the foundation of the world (Rev. 13:8) Chapter 2: Redemption Is Cosmic 20 ● Christ reconciling all things (Col. 1:20) ● Creation’s groan and glory (Rom. 8:19–23) ● The resurrection as the dawning of a new creation Chapter 3: Israel, Covenant, and the Story of Soteria 26 ● The Law and the Prophets pointing to Christ ● Israel’s place in redemptive history ● Christ as the fulfillment of every promise (2 Cor. 1:20) 
PART TWO: The Gospel and the End of the Age Chapter 4: What Really Ended? What Truly Began? 33 ● The Old Covenant dissolved (Heb. 8:13) ● A New Covenant of grace and eternal life (Heb. 13:20) ● Jesus as the pivot of the ages (Gal. 4:4–5) Chapter 5: Rethinking the Olivet Discourse 39 ● Reading Matthew 24, Luke 21 in context ● “This generation” and the fall of Jerusalem (AD 70) ● The end of temple Judaism, not the world Chapter 6: Revelation Revisited – Triumph, Not Terror 45 ● Time indicators: “shortly,” “at hand” (Rev. 1:1; 22:6) ● Symbolism, not literalism ● The victorious Lamb and the overcoming Church Chapter 7: The Kingdom Has Come and Keeps Coming 51 ● Already and not yet of the Kingdom ● Parables of present power (Matt. 13) ● Reigning in the midst of enemies (Ps. 110) 
PART THREE: Salvation in the Now Chapter 8: We Live From Victory, Not For It 58 ● Righteousness as our position (Rom. 5:1) ● Freedom from condemnation (Rom. 8:1) ● Our identity as overcomers (1 John 5:4) 
11

Chapter 9: New Heavens and New Earth – Now and Future 64 ● Heaven and Earth as covenant language ● The Church as the New Jerusalem (Heb. 12:22) ● Seed-form reality growing toward fullness Chapter 10: The Church and the Age to Come 70 ● Salt and light as cultural mandates ● Apostolic mission and kingdom expansion ● The Church as the present steward of divine order 
PART FOUR: Glorification and the Ongoing Hope Chapter 11: Resurrection – Fulfilled Yet Unfolding 77 ● Firstfruits and the bodily hope (1 Cor. 15:20–23) ● Immortality at Christ’s appearing (Phil. 3:20–21) ● The mystery of change Chapter 12: Death Defeated 83 ● Christ’s final blow to death (Heb. 2:14–15) ● Our triumph in His victory (1 Cor. 15:54–57) ● Death as a door, not a defeat Chapter 13: The Restoration of All Things 88 ● Acts 3:21 and covenantal restoration ● The image of God fully restored ● God dwelling with man forever (Rev. 21:3) 
PART FIVE: The Implications of a Victorious Eschatology Chapter 14: Discern the Times, Don’t Fear Them 94 ● Escaping escapism ● Sound prophetic discernment (1 Thess. 5:20–21) ● Prophecy for edification, not panic Chapter 15: Hope, Mission, and Dominion 100 ● The Great Commission in fulfilled context ● Engaging culture as kingdom strategy ● Dominion without dominionism Chapter 16: Living Forward – Anchored in Peace, Moving in Power 106 ● The joy of understanding fulfilled prophecy ● The believer’s confidence in divine timeline ● “Occupy till I come” with eternal perspective 
BONUS APPENDIX : Clarifying the Controversies ● Rapture: History, Hype, and Hope 113 ● Antichrist: One Man or a System? 118 12

● 666 and Nero Caesar: Contextual Arguments 123 ● Millennium Views Simplified 128 ● Understanding the "End Times" - Matthew 24 133 ● Exposition of 2 Peter 3 136 ● Exposition of 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 139 ● Joint Heirs with Christ 144 Closing Exhortation 148

PART ONE: The Eternal Blueprint of Salvation 
 
14

Chapter 1 
Chosen Before Time Began 
Introduction 
The narrative of salvation, as revealed in Scripture, does not emerge as a 
reaction
 
to
 
humanity’s
 
fall
 
into
 
sin
 
but
 
as
 
the
 
grand
 
expression
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose,
 
which
 
was
 
established
 
before
 
the
 
foundations
 
of
 
the
 
world.
 
This
 
foundational
 
understanding
 
is
 
essential
 
for
 
a
 
victorious
 
eschatology,
 
for
 
when
 
we
 
perceive
 
salvation
 
as
 
an
 
eternal
 
plan,
 
we
 
embrace
 
a
 
vision
 
of
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
work
 
that
 
is
 
not
 
contingent
 
on
 
human
 
failure
 
but
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
His
 
sovereign
 
will
 
and
 
preordained
 
love.
 
As
 
we
 
explore
 
this
 
concept
 
in
 
detail,
 
we
 
will
 
examine
 
the
 
theological
 
depth
 
of
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
choice
 
in
 
Christ
 
and
 
how
 
this
 
influences
 
not
 
only
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation
 
but
 
also
 
our
 
expectations
 
of
 
the
 
end
 
times.
 
God’s Eternal Purpose in Christ (Ephesians 1:4–5) 
In Ephesians 1:4-5, Paul writes, "According as he hath chosen us in him 
before
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
world,
 
that
 
we
 
should
 
be
 
holy
 
and
 
without
 
blame
 
before
 
him
 
in
 
love:
 
having
 
predestinated
 
us
 
unto
 
the
 
adoption
 
of
 
children
 
by
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
to
 
himself,
 
according
 
to
 
the
 
good
 
pleasure
 
of
 
his
 
will."
 
This verse is not just a theological statement; it is the cornerstone of our 
understanding
 
of
 
salvation.
 
Paul’s
 
words
 
affirm
 
that
 
salvation
 
is
 
not
 
an
 
afterthought of God but a plan designed in the depths of eternity. From the 
very
 
beginning,
 
before
 
time
 
as
 
we
 
know
 
it
 
even
 
began,
 
God’s
 
intention
 
was
 
to
 
choose
 
us
 
in
 
Christ,
 
to
 
make
 
us
 
holy,
 
and
 
to
 
adopt
 
us
 
as
 
sons
 
and
 
daughters
 
through
 
Jesus.
 
The
 
apostle
 
clearly
 
states
 
that
 
God’s
 
eternal
 
purpose
 
was
 
executed
 
through
 
Christ,
 
establishing
 
a
 
relationship
 
with
 
humanity
 
based
 
on
 
adoption.
 
15

The Eternal Choice : 
God’s
 
election
 
is
 
a
 
profound
 
mystery,
 
why
 
He
 
chose
 
us
 
is
 
not
 
found
 
in
 
any
 
merit
 
of
 
our
 
own
 
but
 
in
 
His
 
divine
 
will
 
and
 
purpose.
 
This
 
concept
 
of
 
divine
 
election,
 
as
 
proposed
 
by
 
Paul,
 
isn’t
 
merely
 
about
 
individuals
 
but
 
speaks
 
to
 
God’s
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
for
 
all
 
of
 
creation.
 
It
 
highlights
 
that
 
salvation
 
was
 
designed
 
to
 
be
 
a
 
process
 
of
 
being
 
"conformed
 
to
 
the
 
image
 
of
 
His
 
Son"
 
(Romans
 
8:29).
 
The
 
choice
 
made
 
before
 
time
 
began
 
was
 
always
 
in
 
Christ,
 
meaning
 
that
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
was
 
not
 
only
 
the
 
means
 
of
 
salvation
 
but
 
also
 
the
 
central
 
figure
 
through
 
whom
 
this
 
eternal
 
plan
 
would
 
unfold.
 
In this context, the doctrine of election is not about individual 
predestination
 
in
 
the
 
narrow
 
sense
 
but
 
about
 
God’s
 
overarching
 
intention
 
to
 
call
 
a
 
people
 
to
 
Himself
 
through
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
holistic
 
vision,
 
God,
 
in
 
His
 
infinite
 
wisdom,
 
predestined
 
a
 
people
 
who
 
would
 
become
 
holy
 
and
 
blameless,
 
achieving
 
this
 
not
 
by
 
their
 
own
 
effort
 
but
 
through
 
Christ’s
 
finished
 
work.

Appendix: 
666 and Nero Caesar: Contextual Arguments 
The number 666, often associated with the Mark of the Beast in Revelation 
13:18,
 
has
 
been
 
a
 
subject
 
of
 
much
 
speculation
 
and
 
interpretation
 
throughout
 
history.
 
While
 
the
 
traditional
 
view
 
tends
 
to
 
identify
 
this
 
number
 
with
 
a
 
future,
 
singular
 
Antichrist
 
figure,
 
a
 
growing
 
body
 
of
 
scholars,
 
especially
 
within
 
the
 
frameworks
 
of
 
preterism
 
and
 
historical
 
interpretation,
 
proposes
 
that
 
666
 
is
 
most
 
directly
 
linked
 
to
 
Nero
 
Caesar,
 
the
 
infamous
 
Roman
 
emperor
 
who
 
reigned
 
from
 
54
 
to
 
68
 
A.D.
 
This
 
appendix
 
explores
 
contextual
 
arguments
 
that
 
suggest
 
Nero
 
is
 
the
 
immediate
 
fulfillment
 
of
 
the
 
666
 
prophecy,
 
grounding
 
this
 
interpretation
 
within
 
the
 
first-century
 
historical
 
context
 
of
 
the
 
book
 
of
 
Revelation.
 
1. The Historical Context of Revelation and Nero Caesar 
The Book of Revelation, written by the apostle John during the reign of 
Domitian
 
(around
 
95-96
 
A.D.),
 
is
 
often
 
understood
 
as
 
a
 
prophetic
 
critique
 
of
 
Roman
 
imperialism
 
and
 
its
 
persecution
 
of
 
early
 
Christians.
 
The
 
book
 
is
 
apocalyptic
 
in
 
genre,
 
using
 
vivid
 
imagery,
 
symbols,
 
and
 
numbers
 
to
 
convey
 
its
 
message.
 
The
 
number
 
666
 
appears
 
specifically
 
in
 
Revelation
 
13:18,
 
where
 
it
 
is
 
described
 
as
 
the
 
number
 
of
 
a
 
man,
 
a
 
cryptic
 
reference
 
that
 
many
 
scholars
 
believe
 
points
 
to
 
a
 
specific
 
historical
 
figure:
 
Nero
 
Caesar.
 
2. Numerical Gematria: The Key to Understanding 666 
The use of numbers as symbols is a hallmark of Jewish apocalyptic 
literature,
 
and
 
the
 
number
 
666
 
is
 
commonly
 
understood
 
as
 
a
 
form
 
of
 
gematria,
 
a
 
system
 
of
 
assigning
 
numerical
 
values
 
to
 
letters
 
in
 
a
 
word.
 
In
 
the
 
ancient
 
world,
 
this
 
was
 
a
 
common
 
practice
 
for
 
encoding
 
secret
 
meanings.
 
In Hebrew and Greek (the original languages of much of the Bible), the 
letters
 
of
 
"Nero
 
Caesar"
 
can
 
be
 
numerically
 
calculated
 
to
 
total
 
666.
 
Here’s
 
how
 
the
 
gematria
 
works:
 
123

● Nero Caesar in Hebrew is written as נרו קסר Neron Kesar). 
● When these letters are assigned numerical values according to the 
system
 
of
 
gematria,
 
the
 
sum
 
of
 
the
 
letters
 
adds
 
up
 
to
 
666.
 
This provides a strong contextual argument that 666 refers directly to Nero 
Caesar,
 
an
 
emperor
 
who
 
was
 
both
 
despised
 
and
 
feared
 
by
 
early
 
Christians
 
for
 
his
 
brutal
 
persecution
 
of
 
believers.
 
He
 
is
 
often
 
remembered
 
for
 
his
 
role
 
in
 
the
 
great
 
fire
 
of
 
Rome
 
in
 
64
 
A.D.
 
and
 
for
 
later
 
blaming
 
Christians
 
for
 
the
 
fire,
 
leading
 
to
 
widespread
 
persecution
 
and
 
martyrdom.
 
3. Nero and the Beast 
In Revelation 13, the beast is described as having a blasphemous mouth 
and
 
a
 
persecuting
 
role
 
against
 
God’s
 
people.
 
This
 
imagery
 
strongly
 
parallels
 
Nero’s
 
reign,
 
during
 
which
 
he
 
persecuted
 
Christians,
 
forced
 
them
 
into
 
public
 
spectacles,
 
and
 
even
 
used
 
them
 
as
 
human
 
torches
 
to
 
light
 
his
 
garden
 
at
 
night.
 
Nero’s connection to the beast also aligns with the description of the beast 
as
 
being
 
both
 
a
 
political
 
figure
 
and
 
one
 
associated
 
with
 
the
 
Roman
 
Empire’s
 
oppressive
 
power.
 
In
 
the
 
context
 
of
 
early
 
Christianity,
 
the
 
Roman
 
Empire
 
itself
 
was
 
often
 
portrayed
 
as
 
the
 
agent
 
of
 
evil
 
in
 
the
 
world,
 
and
 
Nero,
 
as
 
one
 
of
 
its
 
most
 
notorious
 
emperors,
 
became
 
the

4. The Early Christian Interpretation of 666 
Early Christian writings, including the works of Irenaeus, Hippolytus, and 
Tertullian,
 
also
 
make
 
connections
 
between
 
the
 
Antichrist
 
and
 
Nero
 
Caesar.
 
● Irenaeus (c. 130-202 A.D.) is one of the earliest Christian writers

the
 
number
 
666
 
with
 
Nero.

his
 
work
 
Against
 
Heresies
,
 
he
 
writes
 
that
 
the
 
name
 
of
 
the
 
beast,
 
when
 
calculated
 
in
 
Greek,
 
comes
 
to
 
666.
 
His
 
writings
 
reflect
 
the
 
124

post-tribulational view of the Book of Revelation, where the 
prophetic
 
imagery
 
has
 
immediate
 
relevance
 
to
 
Christians
 
experiencing
 
persecution
 
under
 
Nero
 
and
 
other
 
Roman
 
emperors.
 
● Hippolytus (c. 170-235 A.D.), another early Christian scholar, also 
made
 
similar
 
associations,
 
interpreting
 
the
 
beast
 
of
 
Revelation
 
as
 
symbolic
 
of
 
Nero.
 
His
 
writings
 
suggest
 
that
 
Nero

viewed

prefiguration
 
of

final
 
Antichrist
 
who

These early Church Fathers understood the Apocalypse not as a distant

of

timely
 
message

generation,

call
 
to
 
persevere
 
in

of
 
Roman
 
oppression.
 
This
 
perspective
 
is
 
in
 
line
 
with
 
a
 
preterist
 
reading
 
of
 
Revelation,
 
which
 
suggests
 
that
 
much
 
of

content

in

While 666 is often taken as a literal number, it is also important to consider

Revelation 20 speaks of a “thousand years”, a number often taken literally.

in

a

View Key Idea When Does Jesus Return? 
What Is the Millennium? 
Dispensational Premillennialism 
Jesus will return before a literal 1,000-year reign on earth. 
Before the Millennium 
A future, physical kingdom on earth after Jesus returns. 
Historic Premillennialism 
Similar to the above but without the “rapture” idea. 
Before the Millennium 
A future reign of Christ on earth, following a time of tribulation. 
Postmillennialism The Gospel will bring a period of peace and righteousness across the earth before Jesus returns. 
After the Millennium 
A golden age of Gospel influence in history. 
Amillennialism The “1,000 years” is a symbolic number for the present reign of Christ through the Church. 
After a symbolic Millennium 
A spiritual, heavenly reign already in progress.

Just as the beast and the dragon in Revelation aren’t literal creatures, the 
“thousand
 
years”
 
isn’t
 
necessarily
 
a
 
countdown
 
clock.

it
 
pictures
 
a
 
complete
 
period
 
of

reign.

ensures that believers will not be defeated by death but will

in
 
His
 
triumph.
 
2. The Role of the Church in Christ’s Return 
○ The Church is called to participate in Christ's triumph. The 
moment
 
of
 
being
 
caught
 
up
 
to
 
meet
 
Christ
 
in
 
the
 
air
 
is
 
a
 
celebration
 
of
 
the
 
believers’
 
victory
 
in
 
Christ.
 
The
 
church
 
is
 
not
 
waiting
 
to
 
escape
 
the
 
world
 
but
 
to
 
witness
 
Christ’s
 
triumph
 
and
 
usher
 
Him
 
into
 
His
 
eternal
 
reign.
 
This
 
aligns
 
with
 
the
 
teaching
 
of
 
John
 
Chrysostom,
 
who
 
noted
 
that
 
Christ’s
 
return
 
is
 
a
 
victory
 
procession
 
where
 
believers
 
join
 
in
 
celebrating
 
His
 
kingdom’s
 
final
 
establishment
 
(Homilies
 
on
 
1
 
Thessalonians,
 
8).
 
3. The Comfort of the Believer 
○ The ultimate comfort in this passage is the certainty of 
Christ's
 
return
 
and
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
His
 
kingdom.
 
This
 
comfort
 
is
 
not
 
based
 
on
 
escaping
 
suffering
 
but
 
on
 
the
 
promise
 
of
 
victory.
 
As
 
theologian
 
N.T.
 
Wright
 
points
 
out,
 
the
 
Christian
 
hope
 
is
 
not
 
an
 
escape
 
from
 
the
 
world
 
but
 
the
 
transformation
 
of
 
the
 
world
 
through
 
the
 
victory
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
kingdom
 
(Surprised
 
by
 
Hope).
 
Conclusion 
In conclusion, 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 provides believers with a hope that 
is
 
centered
 
on
 
the
 
victorious
 
return
 
of
 
Christ.
 
The
 
moment
 
when
 
believers
 
are
 
“caught
 
up”
 
to
 
meet
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
not
 
about
 
escaping
 
the
 
earth
 
but
 
about
 
participating
 
in
 
Christ’s
 
triumph
 
as
 
He
 
returns
 
to
 
establish
 
His
 
eternal
 
reign.
 
This
 
event
 
marks
 
the
 
consummation
 
of
 
the
 
kingdom
 
of
 
God,
 
where
 
believers
 
will
 
be
 
transformed
 
and
 
united
 
with
 
Christ
 
forever.
 
The
 
comfort
 
we
 
derive
 
from
 
this
 
passage
 
is
 
not
 
only
 
in
 
the
 
future
 
promise
 
of
 
resurrection
 
but
 
also
 
in
 
the
 
present
 
reality
 
of
 
participating
 
in
 
Christ’s
 142

victory. This hope compels us to live boldly in light of the coming 
kingdom,
 
trusting
 
that
 
the
 
King
 
will
 
return
 
in
 
glory
 
and
 
victory.

Appendix 
Joint Heirs with Christ 
A Christ-Centered View of Our Inheritance 
The doctrine of being "joint-heirs with Christ" (Romans 8:17) is a 
profound
 
theological
 
truth
 
that
 
speaks
 
to
 
the
 
believer’s
 
union
 
with
 
Christ
 
in
 
His
 
reign,
 
glory,
 
and
 
inheritance.
 
Unlike
 
the
 
idea
 
of
 
co-heirs,
 
which
 
implies
 
divided
 
portions,
 
joint-heirs
 
signifies
 
shared
 
and
 
equal
 
participation
 
in
 
the
 
full
 
inheritance
 
that
 
belongs
 
to
 
Christ.
 
"And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with 
Christ;
 
if
 
so
 
be
 
that
 
we
 
suffer
 
with
 
him,
 
that
 
we
 
may
 
be
 
also
 
glorified
 
together."-
 
Romans
 
8:17
 
(KJV)
 
This glorious union reveals that the believer is not merely an adopted 
member
 
of
 
God’s
 
household
 
but
 
is
 
positioned
 
to
 
share
 
equally
 
in
 
the
 
Son’s
 
inheritance.
 
As
 
the
 
Bride
 
of
 
Christ,
 
the
 
Church
 
is
 
invited
 
into
 
full
 
participation
 
in
 
the
 
Kingdom,
 
not
 
as
 
servants
 
receiving
 
wages,
 
but
 
as
 
sons
 
and
 
daughters
 
receiving
 
a
 
legacy.
 
Theological and Historical Insights 
1. John Calvin (Reformer) 
“Christ has nothing distinct from His people, for they are 
made
 
one
 
with
 
Him.
 
All
 
that
 
He
 
has,
 
He
 
shares
 
with
 
them…
 
not
 
as
 
a
 
portion,
 
but
 
as
 
a
 
joint
 
possession.”
 
 
-
 
Commentary
 
on
 
Romans
 
8:17
 
2. Matthew Henry (Puritan commentator) 
“They are joint-heirs with Christ, not merely co-heirs 
receiving
 
different
 
lots,
 
but
 
inheriting
 
with
 
Him,
 
in
 
Him,
 
and
 
through
 
Him;
 
for
 
He
 
is
 
the
 
heir
 
of
 
all
 
things.”
 
144

3. N.T. Wright (Contemporary New Testament scholar) 
“To be a joint-heir with Christ is to share in His vocation as 
the
 
true
 
Son
 
of
 
God,
 
to
 
take
 
up
 
the
 
renewed
 
image-bearing
 
role
 
of
 
Adam,
 
now
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
Christ.”
 
 
-
 
Paul
 
and
 
the
 
Faithfulness
 
of
 
God
 
4. Origen of Alexandria (Church Father, 3rd Century) 
“He who is united with Christ becomes as one with Him, not 
in
 
essence,
 
but
 
in
 
dignity
 
and
 
participation
 
in
 
the
 
divine
 
inheritance.”
 
5. Augustine of Hippo (Church Father) 
"He is the heir because He is the Son by nature; we are heirs 
because
 
we
 
are
 
sons
 
by
 
adoption.
 
Yet
 
we
 
inherit
 
not
 
less
 
than
 
He
 
because
 
we
 
are
 
made
 
one
 
with
 
Him."
 
6. John Piper (Reformed Pastor and Author) 
“Being a joint-heir means that the inheritance God gives to His 
Son,
 
everything,
 
is
 
shared
 
with
 
us.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
pie
 
that
 
gets
 
cut
 
into
 
pieces;
 
we
 
all
 
inherit
 
the
 
whole.”
 
Scriptural Foundation 
● Hebrews 1:2 – "...whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by 
whom
 
also
 
he
 
made
 
the
 
worlds."
 
● Psalm 2:8 – "Ask of me, and I shall give thee the heathen for thine 
inheritance,
 
and
 
the
 
uttermost
 
parts
 
of
 
the
 
earth
 
for
 
thy
 
possession."
 
● Romans 8:17 – "And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and 
joint-heirs
 
with
 
Christ..."
 
145

● Ephesians 1:11 – "In whom also we have obtained an 
inheritance…"
 
● Revelation 3:21 – "To him that overcometh will I grant to sit with 
me
 
in
 
my
 
throne…"
 
● 1 Corinthians 3:21-23 – “All things are yours… and ye are 
Christ’s;
 
and
 
Christ
 
is
 
God’s.”
 
Joint Heirs vs. Co-Heirs 
● Co-heirs suggests a division of inheritance, like siblings dividing 
property.
 
● Joint-heirs (Greek: synklēronomoi ) indicates shared ownership 
and
 
equal
 
access
.
 
The
 
entire
 
inheritance
 
belongs
 
fully
 
to
 
Christ
 
and
 
fully
 
to
 
us
 
in
 
Him
,without
 
diminishing
 
either
 
party’s
 
possession.
 
 Illustration: If Christ receives the crown, we don’t get a piece of 
it,we
 
get
 
to
 
wear
 
the
 
crown
 
with
 
Him
 
(Rev.
 
3:21).
 
His
 
throne
 
becomes
 
our
 
throne
 
because
 
we
 
are
 
His
 
Body.
 
In Roman law, to be a joint-heir ( heres in solidum ) meant the entire 
inheritance
 
was
 
entrusted
 
to
 
each
 
party
 
fully.
 
Likewise,
 
our
 
inheritance
 
in
 
Christ
 
is
 
not
 
subdivided,
 
but
 
whole
 
and
 
complete,
 
given
 
to
 
each
 
believer
 
in
 
union
 
with
 
the
 
glorified
 
Christ.
 
Kingdom Implications 
As joint-heirs: 
● We share in His suffering now (Romans 8:17) and will share in His 
glory
 
later.
 
● We will reign with Him (2 Timothy 2:12; Revelation 5:10). 
● We partake in the new creation (Romans 8:19-21). 146

● We inherit eternal life , righteousness , and the world to come . 
Our sonship grants us legal standing; our union with Christ grants us 
relational
 
intimacy
 
and
 
royal
 
participation.
 
The
 
earth
 
was
 
given
 
to
 
Christ
 
as
 
His
 
possession
 
(Psalm
 
2:8),
 
and
 
now,
 
in
 
Christ,
 
it
 
becomes
 
our
 
shared
 
inheritance,
 
not
 
to
 
exploit
 
but
 
to
 
steward
 
and
 
rule
 
under
 
His
 
Lordship.
 
Conclusion 
Being joint-heirs with Christ means we share in all that He has received 
from
 
the
 
Father.
 
This
 
includes
 
authority,
 
glory,
 
inheritance,
 
and
 
ultimately,
 
dominion
 
over
 
the
 
new
 
creation.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
staggering
 
expression
 
of
 
grace
 
and
 
union
 
with
 
Christ.
 
Our
 
inheritance
 
is
 
secure,
 
complete,
 
and
 
eternal
 
because
 
it
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
Christ
 
Himself,
 
the
 
rightful
 
Heir
 
of
 
all
 
things.
 
This doctrine is more than encouragement, it is empowerment. It shapes 
how
 
we
 
live,
 
suffer,
 
serve,
 
and
 
reign.
 
We
 
are
 
heirs
 
not
 
of
 
temporal
 
things
 
only,
 
but
 
of
 
eternal
 
realities.
 
Let
 
us
 
therefore
 
live
 
with
 
confidence,
 
humility,
 
and
 
purpose,
 
knowing
 
we
 
are
 
heirs
 
of
 
a
 
Kingdom
 
that
 
cannot
 
be
 
shaken.
 
147

We have not believed cleverly devised myths.

have
 
not
 
gathered
 
around

of
 
fear
 
or

of

the

reigns
 
now,
 
and

are
 
seated
 
with
 
Him.

is
 
not
 
trying
 
to
 
secure
 
victory;

has
 
already
 
triumphed.
 
SOTERIA, this great salvation, is more than our rescue; it is our

and

in
 
divine

Once

in

as

of

in

About the Book 
Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: Living in Christ’s Victory Today and 
Forever
 
is
 
the
 
third
 
volume
 
in
 
the
 
Soteria
 
series,
 
a
 
journey
 
that
 
began
 
with
 
the
 
wonder
 
of
 
our
 
sonship
 
and
 
redemption,
 
moved
 
through
 
the
 
beauty
 
of
 
rest
 
and
 
divine
 
rhythm,
 
and
 
now
 
arrives
 
at
 
the
 
glorious
 
horizon
 
of
 
our
 
salvation’s
 
consummation.
 
This volume is deeply personal for me. 
It was born out of both a deep study of Scripture and a season of pastoral 
shaking,
 
when
 
teaching
 
about
 
victorious
 
eschatology
 
brought
 
clarity
 
to
 
some
 
and
 
conflict
 
to
 
others.
 
Yet,
 
in
 
the
 
midst
 
of
 
the
 
tension,
 
something
 
unshakable
 
was
 
forged
 
in
 
me:
 
a
 
commitment
 
to
 
proclaim
 
a
 
Christ-centered,
 
hope-filled
 
view
 
of
 
the
 
end,
 
one
 
that
 
magnifies
 
Jesus,
 
not
 
fear.
 
In these pages, I invite you to rethink what you’ve been taught about “the 
last
 
days.”
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
book
 
of
 
gloom
 
and
 
escape
 
but
 
of
 
glory
 
and
 
engagement.
 
We
 
will
 
explore
 
how
 
the
 
great
 
salvation,
 
Soteria
,
 
is
 
not
 
only
 
personal
 
but
 
cosmic,
 
not
 
only
 
past
 
and
 
future,
 
but
 
powerfully
 
present.
 
This book is for anyone who: ● Has wrestled with confusing or fear-based end-time teachings, ● Desires to unlearn limiting narratives and rediscover the victorious 
gospel,
 ● Longs to live confidently in the now-and-forever triumph of Christ. Whether you're a theologian, a pastor, or a passionate believer hungry for 
more,
 
my
 
hope
 
is
 
that
 
this
 
book
 
will
 
strengthen
 
your
 
heart,
 
sharpen
 
your
 
understanding,
 
and
 
stir
 
your
 
affections
 
for
 
Jesus,
 
the
 
Savior,
 
the
 
King,
 
and
 
the
 
fulfillment
 
of
 
all
 
things.
 This isn’t just theology. It’s a call to live the fulfilled life, in Christ’s victory, now and forever.
